allies.rationale_text: Intellectual-humility gives falsifiability the stance needed to expose flaws instead of defend identity.
allies.source_quote: Falsifiability requires intellectual humility - the acknowledging of what you don't know.

allies.rationale_text: First-principles-thinking strips away assumptions and falsifiability pressure-tests whether the rebuilt claim survives contact with reality.
allies.source_quote: First Principles Thinking involves stripping away assumptions and conventional methods to arrive at a set of fundamental facts and desired outcomes. Falsifiability is then used to test the new solutions forged from these fundamental facts.

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation-bias is the psychological enemy of falsifiability because it searches for validating evidence instead of disconfirming tests.
antagonists.source_quote: This bias is the psychological enemy of Falsifiability, as it causes individuals to seek out information that validates their existing beliefs.

antagonists.rationale_text: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes it psychologically difficult to accept the evidence that should kill the original hypothesis.
antagonists.source_quote: People have a strong psychological tendency to remain consistent with prior commitments or interpretations. This mechanism fights against the acceptance of disconfirming evidence required by Falsifiability.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Falsifiability conflicts with confirmation-bias when evidence gathering is shaped by the desire to validate the hypothesis rather than genuinely test it.
structured_tensions.source_quote: falsifiability vs confirmation-bias: falsifiability conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when evidence gathering is shaped by the desire to validate the hypothesis rather than genuinely test whether it can be disproven.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Falsifiability conflicts with commitment-and-consistency-bias when prior commitments make it too costly to accept disconfirming evidence.
structured_tensions.source_quote: falsifiability vs commitment-and-consistency-bias: falsifiability conflicts with commitment-and-consistency-bias (commitment and consistency bias) when the desire to remain consistent with prior commitments makes it psychologically difficult to accept disconfirming evidence that would require abandoning the original hypothesis.

curation_notes.summary: The raw source is strong on explicit allies and direct conflicts, so the file stays close to those named first-order relations.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor relation expansion beyond the explicitly named testing and belief-defense surface.
open_questions: Motivated reasoning and hammer effects remain source-real antagonists, but confirmation and commitment were the cleaner canonical mappings for this conservative pass.
